title: Cat Lump After Vaccination: Causes & When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Discover what causes lumps at a cat's vaccination site, why they are usually low urgency, and how to monitor your pet's health effectively.
content_html:
Understanding Post-Vaccination Lumps

It can be worrying to find a small bump or lump under your cat’s skin after they have received their shots. In most cases, this is a normal localized reaction to the vaccine or the injection process itself. This small, firm, and usually painless mass is typically caused by the body's immune system responding to the components of the vaccine, which is how your cat builds the necessary immunity to stay healthy.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for a new lump at a vaccination site is generally Low. Most of these small bumps are benign and will disappear on their own within two to four weeks. However, you should maintain a watchful eye. It is important to contact your veterinarian if the lump is still present after three months, if it grows larger than two centimeters, or if it continues to increase in size one month after the injection. While rare, a more serious condition called feline injection-site sarcoma can occur, making consistent monitoring essential.

Why Taking a Photo Helps

Documenting the lump with a photo can be incredibly helpful for your veterinarian during a triage assessment. A clear image allows the vet to see the exact location and surface appearance of the area. We recommend taking a photo with a common object, like a coin or a ruler, placed next to the lump to provide a clear sense of scale. This baseline image allows you to compare future photos to see if the lump is shrinking or growing over time.

faqs.question: How long does a vaccine lump typically last?
faqs.answer: Most post-vaccination lumps resolve naturally within 2 to 4 weeks as the initial immune response subsides.

faqs.question: What is the 3-2-1 rule for cat vaccination lumps?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians recommend the 3-2-1 rule: seek medical advice if the lump persists for more than 3 months, is larger than 2 centimeters, or is increasing in size more than 1 month after the shot.

faqs.question: Is it normal for the lump to be warm to the touch?
faqs.answer: Mild warmth and redness can be part of a normal inflammatory response, but if these symptoms are accompanied by discharge or extreme pain, you should contact your vet.
